title: Understand matrices as transformations of the plane
description: Use your understanding of how matrices transform the plane to solve various problems.
topic_path: Matrices as transformations of the plane
Understand matrices as transformations of the plane

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1 & 0
\\\\
0 & 1
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{-1} & \redE 0
\\\\
\tealE 0 & \redE 1
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, the vertical unit vectors stays put while the horizontal unit vector switches sides:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A reflection across $y$-axis has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a reflection across the $y$-axis.

choices.content: A reflection across the $y$-axis
A reflection across the line $y=x$
A rotation about the origin by $90\degree$
A rotation about the origin by $180\degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
0 & 1
\\\\
1 & 0
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE 0 & \redE 1
\\\\
\tealE 1 & \redE 0
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, the unit vectors are swapped:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
y
\\\\
x
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A reflection across the line $y=x$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a reflection across the line $y=x$.

choices.content: A reflection across the $y$-axis
A reflection across the line $y=x$
A rotation about the origin by $90\degree$
A rotation about the origin by $180\degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
0 & -1
\\\\
1 & 0
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{0} & \redE{-1}
\\\\
\tealE 1 & \redE 0
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, the horizontal unit vector becomes the vertical unit vector, and the vertical unit vector becomes opposite to the horizontal unit vector:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-y
\\\\
x
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A rotation about the origin by $90\degree$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a rotation about the origin by $90\degree$.

choices.content: A reflection across the $y$-axis
A reflection across the line $y=x$
A rotation about the origin by $90\degree$
A rotation about the origin by $180\degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1 & 0
\\\\
0 & -1
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{-1} & \redE{0}
\\\\
\tealE 0 & \redE{-1}
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
-1
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, each unit vector is mapped to its opposite:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-x
\\\\
-y
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A rotation about the origin by $180\degree$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a rotation about the origin by $180\degree$.

choices.content: A reflection across the $y$-axis
A reflection across the line $y=x$
A rotation about the origin by $90\degree$
A rotation about the origin by $180\degree$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
3 & 0
\\\\
0 & 3
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{3} & \redE 0
\\\\
\tealE 0 & \redE 3
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
3
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, each unit vector stays in the same direction but becomes $3$ times as long:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
3x
\\\\
3y
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A dilation about the origin by $3$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a dilation about the origin by $3$.

choices.content: A dilation about the origin by $3$
A dilation about the origin by $\dfrac13$
A horizontal stretch about the $y$-axis by $3$
A vertical stretch about the $x$-axis by $\dfrac13$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
\dfrac12 & 0
\\\\
0 & \dfrac12
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{\dfrac12} & \redE 0
\\\\
\tealE 0 & \redE{\dfrac12}
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
\dfrac12
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
\dfrac12
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, each unit vector stays in the same direction but becomes $\dfrac12$ its length:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\dfrac x2
\\\\
\dfrac y2
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A dilation about the origin by $\dfrac12$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a dilation about the origin by $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

choices.content: A dilation about the origin by $2$
A dilation about the origin by $\dfrac12$
A horizontal stretch about the $y$-axis by $2$
A vertical stretch about the $x$-axis by $\dfrac12$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
2 & 0
\\\\
0 & 1
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{2} & \redE 0
\\\\
\tealE 0 & \redE 1
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, the vertical unit vector stays put while the horizontal unit vector stays in the same direction but becomes $2$ times as long:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
2x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A horizontal stretch about the $y$-axis by $2$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a horizontal stretch about the $y$-axis by $2$.

choices.content: A dilation about the origin by $2$
A dilation about the origin by $\dfrac12$
A horizontal stretch about the $y$-axis by $2$
A vertical stretch about the $x$-axis by $\dfrac12$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & 0
\\\\
0 & \dfrac13
\end{array}\right]$

**What is the geometric effect of this transformation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

A transformation matrix on the plane has the same effect on every figure in the plane. Let's think about its effect on a unit square.

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

In our case, the transformation matrix is $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{1} & \redE 0
\\\\
\tealE 0 & \redE{\dfrac13}
\end{array}\right]$.

So we need to think how the grid changes when we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]}$ and we map $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
\dfrac13
\end{array}\right]}$.

Notice that under this transformation, the horizontal unit vector stays put while the vertical unit vector stays in the same direction but becomes $\dfrac13$ its length:

[[☃ image 1]]

So any vector whose coordinates were $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
y
\end{array}\right]$ is now at $\left[\begin{array}{c}
x
\\\\
\dfrac y3
\end{array}\right]$.

Which geometric transformation has that effect?

A vertical stretch about the $x$-axis by $\dfrac13$ has that effect:

[[☃ image 1]]
The transformation is a vertical stretch about the $x$-axis by $\dfrac13$.

choices.content: A dilation about the origin by $3$
A dilation about the origin by $\dfrac13$
A horizontal stretch about the $y$-axis by $3$
A vertical stretch about the $x$-axis by $\dfrac13$

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2 & 3
\\\\
1 & 2
\end{array}\right]$

Below is an interactive graph with the unit square graphed in it.

**Draw the image of the unit square under this transformation.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use these facts to find how the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{-2} & \redE 3
\\\\
\tealE 1 & \redE 2
\end{array}\right]$ maps the unit square. Its image would be the parallelogram formed by the images of the unit vectors.

The transformation maps $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]}$ and $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
3
\\\\
2
\end{array}\right]}$:

[[☃ image 1]]

To complete the unit square, we take the sum of the vectors to find the diagonal connecting the origin with the vertex opposite to it:

$\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]}+\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
3
\\\\
2
\end{array}\right]}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]$

[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of the unit square under the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2 & 3
\\\\
1 & 2
\end{array}\right]$:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & -2
\\\\
-3 & 2
\end{array}\right]$

Below is an interactive graph with the unit square graphed in it.

**Draw the image of the unit square under this transformation.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use these facts to find how the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{1} & \redE{-2}
\\\\
\tealE{-3} & \redE 2
\end{array}\right]$ maps the unit square. Its image would be the parallelogram formed by the images of the unit vectors.

The transformation maps $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]}$ and $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2
\\\\
2
\end{array}\right]}$:

[[☃ image 1]]

To complete the unit square, we take the sum of the vectors to find the diagonal connecting the origin with the vertex opposite to it:

$\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]}+\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-2
\\\\
2
\end{array}\right]}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-1
\\\\
-1
\end{array}\right]$

[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of the unit square under the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & -2
\\\\
-3 & 2
\end{array}\right]$:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
0 & -3
\\\\
-1 & -2
\end{array}\right]$

Below is an interactive graph with the unit square graphed in it.

**Draw the image of the unit square under this transformation.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use these facts to find how the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{0} & \redE{-3}
\\\\
\tealE{-1} & \redE{-2}
\end{array}\right]$ maps the unit square. Its image would be the parallelogram formed by the images of the unit vectors.

The transformation maps $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
-1
\end{array}\right]}$ and $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3
\\\\
-2
\end{array}\right]}$:

[[☃ image 1]]

To complete the unit square, we take the sum of the vectors to find the diagonal connecting the origin with the vertex opposite to it:

$\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
-1
\end{array}\right]}+\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3
\\\\
-2
\end{array}\right]}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
-3
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]$

[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of the unit square under the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0 & -3
\\\\
-1 & -2
\end{array}\right]$:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & 2
\\\\
-3 & 3
\end{array}\right]$

Below is an interactive graph with the unit square graphed in it.

**Draw the image of the unit square under this transformation.**

[[☃ interactive-graph 1]]
hints:
### Key understanding

The transformation matrix $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE a & \redE b
\\\\
\tealE c & \redE d
\end{array}\right]$

1

0

a
\\\\
c
\end{array}\right]}$.

- maps the unit vector $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
b
\\\\
d
\end{array}\right]}$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use these facts to find how the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
\tealE{1} & \redE{2}
\\\\
\tealE{-3} & \redE{3}
\end{array}\right]$ maps the unit square. Its image would be the parallelogram formed by the images of the unit vectors.

1
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$ to $\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]}$ and $\left[\begin{array}{c}
0
\\\\
1
\end{array}\right]$ to $\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]}$:

[[☃ image 1]]

To complete the unit square, we take the sum of the vectors to find the diagonal connecting the origin with the vertex opposite to it:

$\tealE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
1
\\\\
-3
\end{array}\right]}+\redE{\left[\begin{array}{c}
2
\\\\
3
\end{array}\right]}=\left[\begin{array}{c}
3
\\\\
0
\end{array}\right]$

[[☃ image 1]]

This is the image of the unit square under the transformation $\left[\begin{array}{c}
1 & 2
\\\\
-3 & 3
\end{array}\right]$:

[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-7 & 8
\\\\
-5 & 9
\end{array}\right]$

The transformation is performed on the following rectangle:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the area of the image of the rectangle under this transformation?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
### Key understanding

The absolute value of a transformation matrix's determinant gives us the area of the unit square's image under the transformation.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

As a result, the ratio between the area of any image and the area of its pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{\text{area of pre-image}}=\text{absolute value of determinant}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use the given information to substitute some values into this equation and then find the area of the image.

The area of the rectangle is $5\times 8=40$ square units:

[[☃ image 1]]

Now let's find the absolute value of the determinant of the matrix:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left|\det\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
-7 & 8
\\\\
-5 & 9
\end{array}\right]\right)\right|
\\\\
&=|-7\cdot 9-8\cdot (-5)|
\\\\
&=|-23|
\\\\
&=23
\end{align}$

As we said above, the ratio between the area of the image and the area of the pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{40}&=23
\\\\
\text{area of image}&=920
\end{align}$
The area of the image of the rectangle under the transformation is $920$ square units.

options.alt: A Cartesian coordinate plane with a grid has a rectangle. The rectangle is centered horizontally along the x-axis. The x-axis and y-axis range from negative 5 to 5 in increments of 1. The rectangle is outlined in blue, with its vertices located at (negative 3, 3), (5, 3), (5, negative 2), and (negative 3, negative 2). The length and width of the rectangle are 8 and 5 units, respectively.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-6 & 4
\\\\
7 & -8
\end{array}\right]$

The transformation is performed on the following rectangle:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the area of the image of the rectangle under this transformation?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
### Key understanding

The absolute value of a transformation matrix's determinant gives us the area of the unit square's image under the transformation.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

As a result, the ratio between the area of any image and the area of its pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{\text{area of pre-image}}=\text{absolute value of determinant}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use the given information to substitute some values into this equation and then find the area of the image.

The area of the rectangle is $4\times 7=28$ square units:

[[☃ image 1]]

Now let's find the absolute value of the determinant of the matrix:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left|\det\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
-6 & 4
\\\\
7 & -8
\end{array}\right]\right)\right|
\\\\
&=|(-6)(-8)-4\cdot 7|
\\\\
&=|20|
\\\\
&=20
\end{align}$

As we said above, the ratio between the area of the image and the area of the pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{28}&=20
\\\\
\text{area of image}&=560
\end{align}$
The area of the image of the rectangle under the transformation is $560$ square units.

options.alt: A Cartesian coordinate plane with a grid has a rectangle. The rectangle is centered vertically along the y-axis. The x-axis and y-axis range from negative 5 to 5 in increments of 1. The rectangle is outlined in blue, with its vertices located at (negative 3, 4), (1, 4), (1, negative 3), and (negative 3, negative 3). The length and width of the rectangle are 4 and 7 units, respectively.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
5 & 10
\\\\
-8 & 4
\end{array}\right]$

The transformation is performed on the following rectangle:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the area of the image of the rectangle under this transformation?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
### Key understanding

The absolute value of a transformation matrix's determinant gives us the area of the unit square's image under the transformation.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

As a result, the ratio between the area of any image and the area of its pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{\text{area of pre-image}}=\text{absolute value of determinant}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use the given information to substitute some values into this equation and then find the area of the image.

The area of the rectangle is $4\times 9=36$ square units:

[[☃ image 1]]

Now let's find the absolute value of the determinant of the matrix:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left|\det\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
5 & 10
\\\\
-8 & 4
\end{array}\right]\right)\right|
\\\\
&=|5\cdot 4-10(-8)|
\\\\
&=|100|
\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

As we said above, the ratio between the area of the image and the area of the pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{36}&=100
\\\\
\text{area of image}&=3{,}600
\end{align}$
The area of the image of the rectangle under the transformation is $3{,}600$ square units.

options.alt: A Cartesian coordinate plane with a grid has a rectangle. The rectangle is centered horizontally along the x-axis. The x-axis and y-axis range from negative 5 to 5 in increments of 1. The rectangle is outlined in blue, with its vertices located at (negative 4, 4), (5, 4), (5, 0), and (negative 4, 0). The length and width of the rectangle are 9 and 4 units, respectively.

assessment_items.question_content:
Consider this matrix transformation:

$\left[\begin{array}{c}
-7 & -2
\\\\
6 & 12
\end{array}\right]$

The transformation is performed on the following rectangle:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the area of the image of the rectangle under this transformation?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] square units
hints:
### Key understanding

The absolute value of a transformation matrix's determinant gives us the area of the unit square's image under the transformation.

[[☃ explanation 2]]

As a result, the ratio between the area of any image and the area of its pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{\text{area of pre-image}}=\text{absolute value of determinant}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

We can use the given information to substitute some values into this equation and then find the area of the image.

The area of the rectangle is $2\times 5=10$ square units:

[[☃ image 1]]

Now let's find the absolute value of the determinant of the matrix:

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\left|\det\left(\left[\begin{array}{c}
-7 & -2
\\\\
6 & 12
\end{array}\right]\right)\right|
\\\\
&=|-7\cdot 12-(-2)\cdot 6|
\\\\
&=|-72|
\\\\
&=72
\end{align}$

As we said above, the ratio between the area of the image and the area of the pre-image is equal to the absolute value of the determinant:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\text{area of image}}{10}&=72
\\\\
\text{area of image}&=720
\end{align}$
The area of the image of the rectangle under the transformation is $720$ square units.

options.alt: A coordinate plane with the x and y axes labeled. The axes are marked with evenly spaced ticks in increments of 1, ranging from neagtive 5 to 5. A rectangle with two of its sides passing through the points (3,0) and (5,0) measures 2 units by 5 units. The rectangle is 3 units above the x axis and 2 units below the y-axis.
